reason:
Both answers correctly nail the top-line verdict (very worried, existential risk) and converge on the same core playbook: fast detection (telemetry + synthetic monitoring), remote/hot-patchable selector config, resilient selector strategies, checking official APIs, and diversifying acquisition. Technical quality is comparable, with each contributing something the other lacks: A adds a distinct point about Chrome Web Store takedown/policy risk and a Firefox/web-app fallback, while B adds more concrete resilience detail (ytd-* custom element tags as stable anchors, MutationObservers for SPA navigation) and a numeric breakage cadence estimate.

The decisive difference is coverage of the key points list. B explicitly calls out capturing users into a direct relationship ("Convert extension users into direct users fast: get accounts/emails early so a broken extension doesn't mean losing the relationship") as its own action item — one of the four things a competent answer needs to get right. A never states this; its remediation focuses on detection, repair, hardening, and acquisition-channel diversification, but omits the account/email-capture strategy entirely. That's a real gap in an otherwise strong answer, whereas B hits all four key points explicitly.

A's extra material (policy/takedown angle, cross-browser fallback) is useful but doesn't compensate for missing an explicitly-named key point that changes what the asker should build (an accounts/email capture flow), which B provides and A doesn't.
